sentences.text: So my mother and my sister went along, and they saw this man, Chaim Rumkowski.
And there were a lot-- already a lot of women in the same position as my mother.
They had lost their husbands.
Well, we didn't know yet whether they'd been shot.
We were hoping that they were just frightening these people to get money out of them, valuables.
And this Rumkowski, he, himself, didn't know what happened.
He's said, I'll try and find out what happened to these men.
And if they had been shot, at least I'll try.
They should be-- the bodies should be brought back to the ghetto and be buried in a Jewish burial.
That he could never find out.
He made inquiries.
The police, the Lodz police, the German police, told him that this was a special action carried out by the SS.
And it was not under the jurisdiction of the local police.
But we know, from that, two people that were also shot managed to get away from the pits where they buried these bodies.
They were injured.
And they-- they somehow managed to-- they hid.
They stayed there till everything quieted down in that place.
And they came back into the ghetto a few days later.
And they told the story of what happened to these men.
These men were taken.
They were shot.
And they were taken on lorries and taken out-- outside the city, somewhere in a field.
And pits were already dug there, ready for them.
And they were thrown off the lorries into the pits.
And soldiers went around with bayonets, with the bayonets in their rifles.
And whoever was still alive was killed off, with a bayonet, to make sure that they're dead.
But somehow, these two men managed to get away.
And they told the story of what happened.
And to this day, we don't know where they're buried, where this was carried out.
Where they're buried, we don't know.
To this day, I don't know where my father is buried.
I know he was killed that night.
That's all I know.
And--
I did know, but I-- I can't remember.
I can't remember.
They-- they-- They were taken to hospital, there.
I mean they were badly injured, but, somehow, they managed to-- to stay alive.
I don't know the name of these men.
But what I do remember is, after about a week, this Rumkowski managed to-- because we were taken out without any change of clothes, even.
We had nothing to-- to wear.
And so he managed to get two German policeman, from the local police station, and they went along, with my mother and my sister.
My sister was already older than me.
I mean, she-- my mother was very-- you can imagine after such a thing happening.
She was very weak and distraught, so the two of them went along.
And they managed to get some things out from the apartment.
And they came back into the ghetto.
That, he did do for us, Rumkowski.
That's about all he could do.
Yes.
They-- they-- they-- later, when they did go back, they-- they spoke to the caretaker, the Polish caretaker.
And he gave them some information.
He was crying when he told them what happened.
And he told them that all these men were shot.
And that he was pushed back into the apartments and was shot in the apartments.
Then later on, they had to take them out.
This caretaker had to help them take them onto the lorries.
And he was told to clean up the place.
And you couldn't notice anything, any blood or any-- everything was like normal, when they went back, after a week.
And we stayed with my uncle, in that room.
They only lived in one room.
There were-- there were five.
And we were four.
The nine of us, we stayed in that room, until, finally, my mother managed to get a room in a big block, which we had to share.
The four of us had to share with an uncle and an aunt.
So we had a larger part of the room.
We put a curtain through the room.
And there was enough-- only enough space in that room for two beds and a table in the middle.
And when we sat near the table, we had to sit on the beds.
And there was a gas-- not a gas stove, a coal stove, over in the corner, there, somewhere.
And that's all.
There was hardly-- I can't remember.
I don't think there was enough room for a wardrobe, even, in that room.
We had to hang our clothes on-- whatever clothes we did-- we didn't have much clothes.
But whatever we did have, we had to have it hang on the wall, somewhere, on the hooks.
And we stayed in this room, with-- with this uncle and aunt until they were deported.
That was in 1941.
They were taken out in the middle of the night.
And they-- and they were deported.
And in the beginning, this was in a-- in the-- the ghetto closed.
They closed off the ghetto in, I think, in May, 1940.
A few weeks after we came into the ghetto, the ghetto was closed off.
And the Poles were-- the next day, after the shooting, the whole population, the whole Jewish population, from the town, from the city, run into the ghetto, themselves.
That was the idea, you see?
They wanted to speed up this evacuation, so they shot 800 men, that night, and some women, as well, but mainly men.
And the next morning, everybody heard what went on that night before.
So they quickly run into the ghetto.
And so that was on a-- this-- this shooting happened on Thursday.
And on Friday morning, thousands and thousands of people were-- you couldn't use any transport, any public transport.
Or-- Jews weren't allowed to have cars or anything.
So they were dragging, with-- with-- with prams and whatever they could get hold of on to the-- the-- on their backs.
And it was a sight-- terrible.
Never seen anything like it.
Thousands and thousands of people.
And it was very muddy on the ground.
The-- the-- the-- the snow was melting.
And the people dropped things into the mud.
And [SIGHS].
And the Germans were standing and looking on and laughing away.
And they had their revenge and, you know, what-- they-- what is being done to the Jews.
And I must say, some of the Poles also had the-- enjoyed that scenery.
Well, what could we do?
We were-- we were-- we had to-- we had to do.
I mean, the-- the Jews had to move into the ghetto.
They-- they were told, if they don't move quick enough, they will also be shot.
So everybody was terrified.
So it was in-- I think it took about a week for everybody to move in.
And then they-- after about two weeks, they closed-- they fenced around the-- the-- the area of the ghetto, with barbed wire and with watchtowers.
And every 50 yards was a guard, with a machine gun, with a-- it was such a hut, where they stood there, guard post.
And this was us from the outside.
And from inside, they had the Jewish police patrolling, making sure that nobody goes near the-- the fence.
Whoever came near the fence, if he-- if he was getting to near, he was shot.
And so people did-- avoided to go near the fence unless somebody wanted to commit suicide.
There were some people, they committed-- they wanted to commit suicide, so they walked near the fence, and they got shot.
And that's how they closed off the ghetto.
And then, they gave an order that everybody should-- they opened a bank.
[LAUGHS] Couldn't understand what they were opening a bank for.
So we realized the bank was everybody should bring in their valuables, their currency, their foreign currency.
And they exchanged it for ghetto money, money that was printed for the ghetto, only, which was worthless outside the ghetto.
But in the ghetto, you could buy the rations with it, whatever they were.
They-- they started introducing rations.
It took some time till they organized.
Everybody had a ration book.
And you got some food.
But Rumkowski, after collecting all the money, and they handed it over to the Germans, he had to do what he's told.
He took orders from the Germans, and he carried them out.
He was a very faithful-- To this day, nobody understands why he was so-- he did it with such enthusiasm.
He carried out-- he tried to do better than the Germans, even, to carry out the orders.
After he-- people brought in all their variables and everything, after a few months, there was no more.
He realized he had got no more money to buy food.
He had to buy the food from the Germans.
It was-- he exchanged some of the money for food.
Then they stopped bringing in food.
So there was [SIGHS] terrible hunger.
We didn't have-- get any food at all.
We used up-- everybody used up whatever there was in the ghetto.
And there was-- you couldn't get-- the price of-- of-- of bread went up to 100 marks.
You couldn't get any.
It was unbelievable.
For-- for a-- to buy a bread, you had to give away-- people gave away diamonds for a loaf of bread-- whatever-- whoever still had diamonds.
And then he was a very shrewd man, this Rumkowski.
So he suggested a plan to the Germans.
He-- he-- he offered them a plan.
He said, he will open factories in the ghetto.
There were a lot of skilled-- there were 12,000 tailors in the ghetto, that-- when we moved in.
When the ghetto closed, there were 162,000 people, I think, that entered the ghetto when it was closed.
So he opened-- first of all, he opened tailoring factories.
And he employed these people.
And they earned money.
And in that way, with that money, what they-- they produced goods.
And he sold this to the Germans.
And for this, he got paid.
Well, I don't know what they paid him, but they paid him with food.
So some food started coming in to the ghetto.
But there was still a very shortage of food.
And people were protesting.
There were riots in the streets.
In the beginning, we realized we are locked in.
If you like, in a-- in a-- like you have a rat in-- in-- in a, you know, in a cage.
You couldn't do anything.
We realized what's happening to us.
So there were still some strong people, young people, so they started protesting.
And they started beating up the police, the Jewish police, I'm talking about.
Because in the ghetto, you didn't see many Germans, not in the beginning, anyway.
The Jews did the dirty work for them.
So after a time, he formed a special police unit.
Rumkowski formed a special police unit.
And they used the same methods as the Gestapo-- whoever did-- caused any trouble.
So they opened a prison.
And they put them in prison.
And from the prison, they deported them.
Why do you think Rumkowski and his police acted in this way?
[SIGHS] Nobody really knows to this day.
I've read a lot of books.
And I've-- and I've-- and I've spoken to a lot of people that were in the ghetto.
And we think, in the beginning, he thought, in that way, he will save the-- the Jews in the ghetto.
He always used to say, in order to cut-- this was later, when he had-- when they started with the-- with the deportations.
So he said, in order to-- his favorite saying was, in order to save the body, you've got to-- sometimes you have to cut off a limb.
So he-- if they wanted a few thousand people to be deported-- in the beginning, it wasn't like that.
In-- in the beginning, it was-- they started.
They said they need people, for work, outside the ghetto, because there wasn't enough work, yet, in the ghetto.
It wasn't organized.
So they took-- they got hold of young people.
And they said they will take them outside the ghetto for work.
So people registered, because they were starving from hunger.
And they were hoping they would get-- earn some money.
And with that money, they will feed their families.
So that's how it was in the beginning.
They used to take out-- the people used to queue up.
They made huge-- hundreds of people, they're queuing up to be registered, for work, outside the ghetto.
So that went on for a few months.
The people were taken away.
And they sent back letters, with money.
And they said, in the letters, that they were being fed, and the-- the conditions are not bad.
And this went on for a few months.
And then we never heard from these people.
A few of my cousins, they were young, healthy men, and they were taken away, like this.
And we never heard of them.
To this day, we don't-- don't know what happened to them.
But later on, we found out.
Yous see, they-- they took these people.
They started organizing building of these concentration camps.
And that took time.
And they needed men to do it.
So they advertised for carpenters, for metalworkers.
And They took these people away.

And also, in order not to have any trouble, if you take away the young people and the-- the strong people, the-- the ones that are left are weak and-- and the children, so they don't make much trouble.
So I don't know whether Rumkowski knew what's going to happen to these people.
But he also wanted to get rid of the troublemakers.
It was easier for him.
Because, in the beginning, they were rioting.
They were beating up the police.

For instance, if-- if-- if there was a transport, let's say a-- a wagon of bread was being moved from one place to the other, and two policemen were sitting on that wagon, to protect the bread, shouldn't get pinched, so they used to attack the wagon with the police.
And they-- and everybody they ate the bread and took it away.
So he had to put a stop to all this.
So gradually, he got-- he got rid of all these troublemakers.
And things quieted down.
And he organized the factories.
And I would say, in the-- the first year, in 19-- that was in the winter of 1940, about 30,000, 40,000 people were already working in different kinds of factories.
And the rest, he kept saying, he must open factories.
He must open factories.
And I-- it's possible the Germans told him, if the people work, they will be all right.
They won't bother them.
But this was only an illusion.
This was just to keep us quiet.
And it-- it went step by step.
I did-- I did nothing.
I think, in the beginning, there were still-- I think there was-- I was, at that time, almost 13 years old.
You asked me about my bar mitzvah.
My bar mitzvah was in the beginning of the ghetto.
So I-- I was-- I still-- there were still some schools.
I think I still went to school.
They opened schools in the ghetto, Rumkowski.
In town, they-- the Germans closed down all the Jewish schools.
But later on, in the ghetto, Rumkowski opened Jewish schools.
And I think-- I can't remember exactly, but I think I still went to, with my brother and my sisters, well, we went to school, until my sister started working.
She-- yeah, one day, we got a letter from-- from Rumkowski that is offering my mother a job.
And he also-- a very nice letter.
He wrote to her, and he said, he-- he knows that we, our family, are the first victims.
And we lost our father in this action.
And he will look after our family.
And it was a very moving letter to my mother.
And-- so, in that letter, he offered my mother a job.
But she wasn't up to it.
She was still very-- she wasn't strong enough to go to work.
So my sister took on this job instead of my mother.
She was, at the time, 16.
She started work in a co-op.
It was-- it was called a co-op, but they distributed the food.
So she did get some food, herself, while she was there.
She wasn't allowed to take anything out.
But while she was there, she could get a slice of bread and whatever, a few potatoes, whatever it was.
But Rumkowski was a very clever chap.
By giving a bread, a loaf of bread, for the week-- no.
It-- it started off that you collected your ration every day, every second day.
So people were busy queuing up to get their ration of bread.
And also, the co-ops, they used to pinch, from every loaf of bread, they pinched a little slice.
Because if you divide it into six or eight portions, you can pinch a little slice.
So there was a lot of pinching going on.
And he wanted to put a stop to this.
So he gave an order, you mustn't cut into a loaf.
You've got to give the whole loaf, to each person, for the week, for seven days, a loaf, which was a very good idea.
Because you couldn't pinch anything.
The people that distributed bread couldn't pinch.
But if a person got a loaf of bread, for a whole week, and he was starving, not everybody could hold himself back and keep the bread for the seven days.
So they finished a loaf in-- in two or three days.
And the rest of the week, they were starving.
So I managed to control myself.
My younger brother, he-- he couldn't.
He-- his bread-- his ration, he was finished with it after a few days.
So my mother had to give it, from her ration, to subsidize his ration, he should.
Because he had-- otherwise, he would have starved, completely.
Anyway, the-- the starvation rate went up, very high, in-- in 1940.
And there was literally hundreds of people dying, every day, from hunger.
And then other trouble started.
Diarrhea, dysentery, typhoid started.
And there were no medicines coming in.
There were doctors, but they couldn't do anything.
They had no medicines.
So it was terrible.
I-- that's when I lost my grandparents, in that winter, in-- in the winter of 1940-41.
And this went on for a few months until worse things came-- they happened-- started happening.
We-- there were rumors going around that people are going to be deported.

And they-- they told us stories that, as there is not enough work in the ghetto, so these people are being deported to work on farms, in different places, in different-- In Germany, they needed people.
As the Germans were taken all into the-- into the army, so they needed people to carry on in the factories.
So they're taking people, from the ghetto, to work in these factories, on the farms.
Whether we believed it or not, we had no choice.
We had to believe it.
There was not much we could do about it.
My uncle and aunt, they were not working.
But one night, in the-- in the winter of 19-- it must have been the beginning of 1941.
And the-- the police knocked in the middle of the night.
And they were told to dress.
And they were taken away.
And we never heard of them again.
They were two young people in their 30s.
They were taken away.
And then we occupied the whole room, because they shared the room with us.
So after they were taken away, we didn't even-- And at such times, when they left, we kissed them.
But they didn't even cry when they went away.
You get so hardened.
It's-- it's-- it's-- it's very hard to understand under normal circumstances.
They were taken out in the middle of the night.
And we never heard from them again.
And then I-- I started work.
Rumkowski opened a factory for boys at-- of my age.
I was, at the time, already 14.
And I started working in a-- in a tailoring factory where we reproduced uniforms for the army.
And we had grown ups as supervisors that taught us how to do it.
And I worked on the sewing machine.
And my mother and my younger brother were not working yet.
One day, we received a letter.
We knew already what the letter is going to contain.
They called them wedding invitations in the ghetto.

When we opened the letter, it said that my brother and my-- and my mother should report to be evacuated and deported, should report to a assembly place, where they will be deported, because they were not working.
When my mother got this letter, she sat down, and she wrote a very nice letter.
She-- she could write a very good letter.
And in the morning, she got up, very early.
She knew where Rumkowski travels to his office.
He-- Jews were not allowed to use any mechanical transport in the ghetto.
Even he didn't-- wasn't allowed to use a car.
He had to have a horse-driven carriage.
So she went along the-- the route where his carriage would pass, and she threw the letter onto the carriage.
And the horses, they-- they-- they-- they jumped.
She was nearly killed that morning.
Anyway, she could see that his-- his secretary-- the man was sitting next to him-- picked up this letter.
After a few days, I must say, we got a reply, from-- signed by Rumkowski, dictated by him and signed, that he's taken note of the letter.
And he understands.
He-- that-- that-- my mother wrote that he promised he would look after our family.
And so he said, he can-- he will let one of the two that was going to be deported go free.
But one of them has to go, has to be deported.
So it was a choice between my mother and my younger brother.
So we decided-- we-- they decided that night.
We were sitting and talking about it and discussing it, who should go, my mother or my brother.
We decided that my younger brother should go.
